sections.paragraph:
Gimel (גִּימֶל), the third letter of the alphabet, when used as a numeral, i.q. three. Its name differs only in form from גָּמָל camel; and its figure in the Phœnician monuments (), on the coins of the Maccabees, and in the Ethiopic alphabet (ገ), bears a resemblance to the neck of the camel. The Greeks received this letter from the Phœnicians, and by turning the head to the right, made it Γ.

As being the softest of the palatals (גיכק) except Yod, it is often interchanged with the harder ones כ and ק; both within the limits of the Hebrew language itself, and as found by a comparison with cognate languages, see גָּדִישׁ , جُدْسُ, كَدِيسُ a heap of sheaves; גָּפְרִית, ܟܶܦܪܬܺܐܳ, كِبْرِيتُ sulphur; גָּנַן and כָּנַן to cover, to protect; גָּנַז and כָּנס, كنز to collect, to heap up; רָגַל and רָכַל to run up and down; גָּבִיעַ, قُبْمةُ calix of flowers; גָּדַד, جَدَّ and قَدَّ to cut; שָׁקַד, שְׁגַד, ܫܶܓܕܳܐ almond.

More rarely it passes over-(2) into gutturals, which are less allied; namely, ע see גָּרָה , גָּמַץ , and , as אֶפְרֹחַ, ܦܰܪܘܽܓܳܐ young of birds, comp. אָחַח.

גֵּא adj. (for גֵּאֶה, from the root גָּאָה), m. proud, arrogant, Isa 16:6.
